sentences.text: So the uprising was finished.
And what were we going to do?
The German went from house to house and had everybody out.
If nobody answered-- if there were small houses, people were in cellar.
So they could very quick get them out.
If nobody answered, they throw a bomb in, and the people just vanished there.
If it was a big house, they waited.
I don't know for how long or how short interval.
And if the people didn't get out, they didn't have the people they wanted, they just put bigger bombs in.
Very often, people came out.
And they were just in a bad mood, the Germans.
They just shot them.
But they didn't shoot us.
We came out.
And I think Mrs.
Stelmachowska said that we are only women here.
There is no men.
There was only Jan.
There was another, I think, young boy and-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to let us go.
But they didn't let us go.
We had to go to a, a certain place, what was called in German, Sammelplatz.
I don't know what it is called in-- I heard it hundreds of times in, in English.
But I forgot.
There everybody was brought to.
And then they marched us to Pruszkow.
Pruszkow-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I think a part of the-- of the way, we marched.
And a part, we went in trucks, in those cattle trucks.
We saw the burning Warsaw all the time.
It was really-- they just-- every house was completely destroyed.
So we arrived in Pruszkow, which was-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it wasn't a camp where you stayed.
They sorted people out there.
Would you call it a transit camp?
A transit camp.
But transit camp you could stay sometimes.
But that was not for staying.
That, they decided what to do with these people.
And in the meantime, we lost Mrs.
Stelmachowska because I think she wanted to go straight to the county.
But in Pruszkow we met again with her.
And Ola Wysocka was in the country again because it was holiday.
She was with her godmother.
So she wasn't when the uprising was going on.
So they sorted us out-- men separate, women separate, old separate, young separate, children separate, and so on.
And when it came to me and children and women separate, I just didn't give in.
And I-- that we knew we go-- they wanted the young and strong one for hard labor in-- well, for labor in Germany.
So I told this soldier that I want my child with me.
And he is very-- he will work with me.
And OK.
I was left with Jan.
Mrs.
Stelmachowska went-- had some family in the country.
And when she said where she is going, they left her to go.
So a woman with, with a girl, and, I think, her old-- his old mother was with them.
They were not very interested.
They were more interested in young people.
And they took me and lots of others somewhere into the country, near Kielce.
It was not Kielce the town.
But it was a small village.
Even-- perhaps it wasn't even a village, some-- in the county where peasants were.
And I stayed there with Jan and worked with these peasants.
I peeled potatoes all the time.
I don't know what we did with them, with all these potatoes.
Jan did also some work.
I don't know what he did, nothing very heavy.
We slept on straw.
And I knew that then one day, the German will come and take us where they wanted to have us.
Perhaps they didn't have any, any idea where people are needed.
So we stayed there for not long, perhaps a week, two weeks near Kielce.
I don't know.
I have to look it up in the-- on the atlas.
I have no idea.
I didn't know much about geography in Poland.
Anyway, it, it was just-- we were not hungry because soup.
It was soup, soup, soup.
And it was cold, but somehow we managed to-- they had always a fire going.
There was a big kitchen.
The peasant had enough wood to heat the kitchen.
And then one day, the German came and we got-- took us to Kielce.
And there, we had the bath, you know, to-- not to have lice, to be unloused.
They were very, very afraid of typhoid.
And and lice can bring typhoid.
So-- and then we got-- they ask what we can do.
They were not too bad, those Germans, if I can say that.
Well, they didn't beat us.
Anyway, I was a Polish woman, and they didn't beat Polish women up on the whole.
But I'll say it-- well, I can make shoes.
They didn't need a shoemaker.
They-- I said I can work in a hospital.
They didn't take Poles to work in a hospital where they had German, German soldiers.
So-- but you say it-- but you speak well German, because I spoke German to them.
So can you translate?
I said, naturally, I can translate, whatever.
Not from very many languages, but I could certainly Polish, German, Polish.
I could French a bit because I learned French at school.
I thought there won't be much to translate anyway.
And Jan, well, Jan was-- I don't know what he had on his-- on his [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] certificate.
He also had the same certificate as I.
That-- in Kielce, and we were unloused.
And we were workers.
And we were going to Ludwigsburg.
It was Ludwigsburg on that-- on this paper.
And we got on a train.
I can't-- it was certainly a, a cattle truck.
It was not an ordinary train.
And--
I can't remember-- no luxury, certainly.
But I can't remember.
And we came to Ludwigsburg and were-- went at once, we-- there were barracks.
And there were already lots of workers.
They were called Fremdenverkehrs.
They were aliens. 'Fremd' is, is-- Fremd is alien.
And so we were Fremdenverkehrs.
And there were a lot.
There was a lot of Poles there because I think they had all these transports from Kielce.
And next day, we got something to eat.
I don't know where and don't know what.
But we got something to eat.
And next day, we were shown-- we were in-- we were marched off in twos or in threes to the factory which was underneath-- underground-- the factory.
And we did some little metal things.
I don't know what it was for.
Naturally, they didn't tell us what it was for.
I didn't-- I had no idea.
Jan explained later to me that it was done for, for airplanes.
Never mind what it was done for.
They had a few days of this work.
It was not hard work.
We didn't have to carry big things, just sitting and doing these movements.
And Jan did it.
He was sitting next to me.
And one [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] evening, I think it was-- or was it early morning-- there was a big what the German called Grossangriff.
That means a heavy bombardment.
It-- all the time it was bombarded, but that was a heavy bombardment.
And our barracks just went.
And we were under the-- just-- the barracks were not very-- they were no-- they were just wood.
Good that they didn't throw bombs which were fire bombs.
But they were just destroyed.
And we climbed out from under the, the wood and whatever there was, helped others out.
And there was chaos, completely chaos, because the factory was also bombed.
And people are running to and fro, and shouting, and crying, and giving, giving orders.
And, you know, I don't know whether you know the German.
But anyway, they liked giving orders.
And I didn't take to these orders very much.
And so I told Jan, off we go.
And we walked off.
We had-- I had the documents in a little bag on, on me.
So I had the documents always with me.
So if I was told where I am-- who I am, I could show them.
And I told Jan, we are going to Austria, not Vienna, but somewhere in the country.
Yes, well, I think Jan explained it to me where that is.
But I had-- I must have an idea.
Anyway, we went south.
We went south.
You have no idea what state Germany was in.
It was quite extraordinary.
My feelings-- because I waited for the terrible-- those Grossangriffe, I waited for them.
But naturally, I knew that I can be killed and Jan can be killed also.
They didn't know that there is a Polish woman walking around during the train they were bombarding because we were partly-- there were trains.
So we boarded a train.
But hundreds of other women with children or with old people, or old people with babies boarded trains.
German were in chaos.
It was already late autumn, '44.
So sometimes, we got something to eat.
There were kind of canteens.
They were free.
You had not to pay because they were for all these people who just walked around some, not knowing what's going on, you know.
I remember once there was a kind of canteen.
And they had some soup, but I had nothing to take the soup in, you know.
And it was terrible.
There was the soup, and I couldn't get it.
But somebody gave me a tin.
So I got some.
And off we went again.
And we took a train.
And we were hiding under-- when the bombs fell, somewhere in cellars.
Where everybody went, we went.
We went out with them.
We went through-- I thought it was Augsburg, but Jan told me it was Ulm.
And that was a real Grossangriff.
And they throw phosphor bombs.
And the whole town, the whole pavements were in flames.
They were burning.
That was really terrible because you couldn't walk.
Well, anyway, so we went and went.
And we're hungry, and we are cold.
And we are-- and went into a train and went out of a train.
And I talked to people.
I told everybody that I was in a train going for-- to a camp.
And the train was bombarded.
And it was just chaos.
And we went out.
So I came to Bregenz because the train we were in went to Bregenz.
So I went out in Bregenz.
Where was Bregenz?
Bregenz is in-- is on the-- on the border of Austria and, and Vorarlberg, I think.
So anyway, it was Vorarlberg, Bregenz.
I may be wrong.
You have to excuse, I don't know geography very well.
So what will I do there in Bregenz?
I went to the job center.
There was everywhere job centers for-- but not for Fremdenarbeiter.
They didn't go for-- looking for work.
They tried to run away.
But I wanted work.
I had the child with me.
So I went there.
And told them the whole story, that I was in the train.
And I showed them my documents, that I-- we were going to Ludwigsburg.
And it was bombarded.
And we went out and couldn't find our way.
And somebody put us in a train.
And it came to Bregenz.
Here I am.
I want to work.
I think they were very impressed by that.
Jan was looking at me, like, his mother is lying so much and so easy.
That was not very nice.
But anyway, again, what can I do?
So I wanted to go into the country to peasants.
I could get milk for Jan and something to eat, even if there was only apples.
Vorarlberg was very famous, that I knew, for apples.
So, you know, they don't-- can I?
Yes.
Can I-- was that my telephone?
No, sorry.
Can I milk a cow?
No, I can't.
That I couldn't.
So can I get into a hospital?
I can do a lot of cleaning things.
And so-- no, they don't want Fremdenverkehr in, in a hospital.
So what can we do with you, woman?
They gave me something to eat, certainly.
They were nice.
They were already-- Austrians, Austrians are the worst, I can tell you.
But these were quite nice people.
Well, they were nice for me then.
And so I said, I can make shoes.
Oh, shoes?
Wonderful.
And at once, they phoned in Gotzis.
That is a little town.
Well, in Vorarlberg, I don't know now whether it's very near, but the whole Vorarlberg is very small.
And there was a [SPEAKING GERMAN].
And yes, they want workers very much.
And can she has the child with her?
Yes, she can have the child with her.
And they have a camp for the Fremdenarbeiter where I can live.
And we'll go into the camp.
So they put-- they gave me again something to eat.
They put me on a train.
And I went to Gotzis.
And there, a German woman was waiting for me.
And it was evening.
And she took me in.
And this woman had four sons, all SS men.
And she was very proud of them.
They were all tall.
She showed me the photographs.
Naturally, I admired them.
We got a wonderful meal.
I had the first bath since I don't know how long.
We had a bath.
Jan had a bath.
She gave me a gray flannel nightshirt with long sleeves.
And Jan, I don't know what, what he got.
She had also grandchildren, so Jan got something.
And we went to a real bed with a big feather bed.
Well, that was--
So next day, I had to go to the camp.
I was transferred in Gotzis.
They had, in Gotzis, a camp where they were nearly all Ukraines and people from [NON-ENGLISH].
And they hated Poles like the worst.
They were the worst Poles-hater hater in-in the world.
And there I was a Polish woman.
And from the beginning, it was-- well, I was taken to the factory and told what to do.
I got a number, as they didn't know my-- couldn't pronounce or didn't want to pronounce my name.
They called me Frau Hundert-- that means woman hundred-- and showed me what to do.
Jan got a bicycle to be a messenger between the factory and the office and the, the-- whatever they needed.
Well, he was very happy about that.
That was nice.
And I started work there and found it very-- not easy, but I, I could do it.
And I-- there were a lot of girls from Liechtenstein, which is next to Vorarlberg.
They worked.
They were very well paid.
They were brought every day by car, by a lorry, to work.
They got ration books, which they didn't need because in Liechtenstein, they had more food than they could eat.
And they were well paid.
So we-- they were on piecework.
And I just had to work.
I had a very nice neighbor there sitting next to me, [NON-ENGLISH], a nice girl from Liechtenstein.
So I made shoes for her.
I made one for the factory and one for her.
So she got more money for the shoes.
And for me, it was-- well, so I was a lazy worker.
But I worked very well.
So-- and I was quite liked by the overseer there.
The Ukraines in the camp were very unpleasant.
And I had a tough time with them.
So I complained once to this overseer in the-- in the factory.
And he told it in the office.
And again, in the office there was the men-- the clerk in the office was an SS man.
He was a Nazi like anything.
He liked me for some reasons.
Perhaps he had pity for me, I don't know.
He was a cripple.
He had polio when he was a child.
That's why he wasn't in the army.
He was a clerk there.
And I said something once, that it's really hard for me to be there.
The child is badly treated, bad language, and so on.
And he arranged for me to get a little room in a Gasthof.
So I got with-- to the Gasthof from the camp.
But the Ukraines still didn't get-- didn't give up.
We had little boxes where we had to clock in in the morning and clock out when we went.
One day, I found a card there.
We know that you are Jewish.
And if you don't disappear, the Gestapo will hear about you.
I knew who wrote it.
It was written in very bad Polish.
It was written in Polish, but by Ukraine.
And I knew who it was.
So I took this card and went to this overseer and told him that I found.
And I don't know what that means.
Well, I know what it means.
It was in Polish, so I translated it in German.
And why somebody thinks that I am a Jewish woman with a Jewish child, I can't imagine.
I am Polish by birth, by marriage, and by whatever you want.
And during the lunch hour, he called all the workers from the-- from the factory together and said he was informed that there are unpleasant things going on.
You are here to work and not to make accusations which may be true or not true, I don't care.
As long as you work, you are not-- I don't know whether he said safe here, but you are needed.
So back to work.
And no more nonsense, more or less that.
And that was the end of my-- that was my second blackmail.
The one was in Warsaw.
And that was the second one.
And I'm glad to say, I think it was the last.
So life went on there.
I was in the Gasthof in this little room with Jan.
Jan was very much liked by all the girls from Liechtenstein.
They brought him apples.
They brought him Bauernbrot.
It is a special bread with, with pears in it.
They gave him a ration card so he could buy something.
I had some money because I was paid very little, but I could buy something.
So when we had these extra ration card, it was very good.
And-- but I worked for Gotzis very eagerly.
And at the hotel, the German didn't bother me anymore.
They were-- I was a Polish woman.
I was a woman with a child.
I worked well.
I behaved well.
I didn't make any, any something.
I kept quiet.
And in the Gasthof, there was a waitress.
Ida was her name.
She invited Jan and me one day on Sunday, when she had a free afternoon, to her parents, who were very simple people.
He worked at the railway.
I don't know what you called it when they pushed the--
Pardon?
A signalman, yes.
He was a signalman.
They were very anti-Nazi.
But naturally, they didn't say anything.
I didn't say anything.
We got some kind of coffee ersatz there and piece of cake.
And we talked.
And it was nice to be with a family because I was terribly alone.
I was only with a child.
That was not real company, was it?
I never spoke to grown-ups.
So here I was in a company.
There was the father, the mother, the, the girl.
The brother was in the army.
They knew very well that Germany's kaput-- never said a thing.
When we got home to the Gasthof, we always got a piece of cake with us.
And that-- after, after the war, Jan visited them.
He was there.
So--
Are we still in the autumn of '44 now or--
That-- oh, no.
The--
Autumn '44 was the uprising in Warsaw.
We are in winter '45.
Winter '45, the beginning of '45.
Yes.
I came just before Christmas.
I got even some presents from the-- from the factory for Christmas.
I got some warm thing.
Jan got a ski outfit, very warm, and shoes, real shoes, not holes.
Because I made wooden shoes, not real shoes, wooden shoes.
They were for prisoners.
So, anyway, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] life went on quiet.
I didn't talk to anybody-- in the hotel, sometimes to Ida, and to the owner, who was a great Nazi.
But I was-- I was told to stay in this-- in this room.
And he had to give me a room.
So there was no question about it.
Jan played chess by himself.
I made him out-- well, the same out of, of, of paper and of, of cotton, a chess set.
So sometimes, German came and played with him.
He was a little boy, but he played chess very well.
So he beat sometimes the German.
So-- and then, one day, it was the 8th of May, '45, everything was quiet.
We heard sometimes airplanes going over us, never bombs, but very, very low-flying.
They flew from Italy, I think, to Germany.
They had the base in Italy somewhere.
Italy was already freed.
Well, we didn't know about that.
So the Moroccans marched in-- black people, the first black people I saw in my life.
I never saw a black person.
And we were free.
We could do what we wanted.
The German were our prisoners now.
And I think we did the best of it.
I-- the Moroccans got at once into this Gasthof.
There were a lot of Gasthof.
They throw the Germans out.
I got a bigger room because I was not German.
I was a Pole.
They made their canteen in this Gasthof, you know, for all the officers.
And a funny thing happened, that the officer in charge of this canteen fell in love with me.
And I didn't know what to do because he really-- he gave me whatever I wanted.
Well, I, I wanted only food, so Jan could have-- Jan was happy.
But I kept Jan very near me.
Not that I was afraid that he will do something to me, but it was a bit unpleasant.
He was very elegant, very good-looking, black, black like--
And you could speak French with him because he was Moroccan--
Well--
[SIGHS] --a little bit.
But anyway, he wanted to marry me and said that when, when I marry him, I will get very fat because they-- all women in Morocco have to be fat.
And I will have all teeth out and will get all gold teeth because that's what they did.
All, all these officers had all gold teeth.
The German made a fortune out of it because they had the-- had the gold from the Jews.
And they had to give it to the Moroccans.
And they had golden teeth made.
Well anyway, I got not involved with this man.
I was very friendly to him, kept Jan near to me, and tried now to do something with my life.
Somebody from the Polish Red Cross arrived, asked me whether I would like to be in charge of the community there.
Because suddenly, a lot of Poles came from the country.
And they didn't know what to do.
They couldn't write.
They couldn't read.
They didn't know very much, so I had to make a list of them.
The Ukraines came to be also on the list.
Not one Ukraine was on the list.
They suddenly were all Poles.
They came from Poland.
They had Galiciana on their-- on their passports, or on their identity cards.
There was not such a thing.
You were either Pole or Ukraine.
There were no Galiciana.
So no Ukraine, but we got very good-- very good-- I'm tired now-- boxes from the Red Cross with fruit-- with food, fruit and also some kind of clothes.
And-- but that was not enough for me.
I had to do something with my life.
I met there-- they came somewhere from the country, but they were not peasants.
They were very educated people-- a Mr. and Mrs.
[NON-ENGLISH].
He had only arm.
He lost his arm during the First World War in Russia.
They were-- they came from Russia somewhere.
And I got very friendly with them.
And I wanted to find my husband.
I knew if-- the one thing I heard once that he's in England.
So Jan had the bicycle for himself now.
So he went around everywhere.
And he had a cart with him where it was written, I'm looking for Dr.
Maximilian Schutz, captain in the Polish Army, x-ray specialist from Lwow, wanted by Mrs.
Eva, Eva Sarnecka in Gotzis.
